Indicator: Use of clean water for meal preparation
Metric: % HH using clean water to prepare food
References for threshold:
Based on WHO/UNICEF guidance on safe food preparation and WASH–nutrition linkages. Threshold reflects widespread use of unsafe water for food preparation.
https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789241594639

Indicator: Use of fuel to cook food
Metric: % HH not using fuel source to prepare food
% HH with safe storage equipment
MSNA indicator: % of households by reported food storage method

Indicator: Qualitative reports of food sharing practices
Metric: Qualitative reports of HHs sharing food with other houseohlds or individuals, especially received assistance

Indicator: Use of surface water in food preparation
Metric: Qualitative reports of surface water use for food preparation
% of households with at least two water containers (one for storage and one for collection)
References for threshold: No global threshold, often referenced in WASH programming guidance.

Indicator: Cost of water containers, treatment materials, or other items related to water utilization in the market
Metric: % change in price of water containers

Indicator: Cost of water containers, treatment materials, or other items related to water utilization in the market
Metric: % change in water treatment materials

Indicator: Cost of water containers, treatment materials, or other items related to water utilization in the market
Metric: Water containers not available in market

Indicator: Cost of water containers, treatment materials, or other items related to water utilization in the market
Metric: Water treatment materials not available in market

Indicator: Qual reports of struggles with water treatment
Metric: Indications that MOST of the popluation may be facing serious challenges with water utilization due to lack of options to treat water (drinking unsafe water directly, lack of fuel to boil water, etc.) and that it is putting them at heightened risk for water borne disease transmission

Indicator: Qual reports of struggles with water storage
Metric: Indications that MOST of the popluation may be facing serious challenges with water utilization due to lack of water storage capacity (water consumption and use limited by number of containers, utilizing unclean/contaminated or broken water containers, lack of water containers in the market, etc.) and that it is putting them at heightened risk for water borne disease transmission
IPC AFI phase
Remarks/Limitations: FEWSNET phases, or Cadre Harmonisé phases can also be used as equivalent
References for threshold: IPC AFI Classification (Phase 4 = Emergency, Phase 5 = Castarophe/Famine)

Metric: % of people in CARI P4
References for threshold: Based on WFP CARI classification; threshold adapted to reflect a substantial share of the population in severe food insecurity (Phase 4), aligned with 20% rule from the IPC Guidelines.

Metric: % of HHs reporting severe or very severe hunger
MSNA indicator: Household Hunger Scale
Remarks/Limitations: Can use PHU4IMPACT functions to compute the composite scores
References for threshold: ANA analytical threshold for area-level flagging, based on IPC AFI Reference table: HHS 4 corresponds with IPC AFI Phase 4 (Emergency), HHS 5-6 corresponds with IPC AFI Phase 5 (Catastrophe/Famine)

Metric: % of HHs reporting very severe hunger
MSNA indicator: Household Hunger Scale
Remarks/Limitations: Can use PHU4IMPACT functions to compute the composite scores
References for threshold: ANA analytical threshold for area-level flagging, based on IPC AFI Reference table: HHS 4 corresponds with IPC AFI Phase 4 (Emergency), HHS 5-6 corresponds with IPC AFI Phase 5 (Catastrophe/Famine)

Metric: % of HHs reporting high consumption-based coping
MSNA indicator: Reduced Coping Strategies Index
Remarks/Limitations: Can use PHU4IMPACT functions to compute the composite scores
References for threshold: ANA analytical threshold for area-level flagging. WFP typical benchmark / IPC reference table lists an rCSI over 19 as crisis/emergency conditions. Value here is reflecting high proportion of population engaging in high coping.

Metric: % of HHs scoring poor FCS score
MSNA indicator: Food Consumption Score
Remarks/Limitations: Can use PHU4IMPACT functions to compute the composite scores
References for threshold: ANA analytical threshold for area-level flagging. WFP Standard threshold for poor FCS is ≤21. Value of 20% aligned with "20% rule" from the IPC guidelines.

Metric: % of localities experiencing severe or extreme hunger
References for threshold: No global threshold exists. ANA analytical hreshold based on alignment with IPC severity logic.

Metric: Qualitative reports of people (reasonable share per unit of analysis) not eating for a few days (2-4 days) visible due to overall catastrophic conditions within the unit of analysis. Other context- specific indications can include: people tying a rope or piece of cloth around their waist to stop hunger pangs, extreme reliance on wild foods, or extreme reliance on social networks (incl. begging)
References for threshold: No global threshold exists. ANA analytical hreshold based on alignment with IPC severity logic.

Indicator: Qualitative reports of mainly children eating
Metric: Qualitative reports indicating that adults within a household are skipping meals or reducing their food consumption to favor children's or pregnant and lactating women's food consumption
References for threshold: No global threshold exists. ANA analytical hreshold based on alignment with IPC severity logic.

Metric: % of HHS reporting a low dietary diversity score
MSNA indicator: Household Food Consumption (HDDS)
References for threshold: HDDS of 0-2 food groups aligns with IPC AFI Phase 4 or 5. No universally agreed global threshold for % of HHs, this ANA analytical threshold is based on alignment with IPC severity logic, 40% indicating a widespread issue.

Indicator: Quantity of total water per capita
Metric: Median litres total water per person per day
Remarks/Limitations: Low total water = signal of system stress/collapse
References for threshold: Shpere minimum standard is 15L/person/day for all uses. ANA threshold reflects severe shorfall (<50% of minimum standard), consistent with emergency conditions

Indicator: Quantity of drinking and cooking water per capita
Metric: Median litres drinking/cooking water per person per day
Remarks/Limitations:
Low drinking water indicates potential survival risk
Low domestic water indicates hygiene breakdown, potential disease risk
References for threshold: Sphere minimum standard is 6L/person/day for drinking + cooking. ANA threshold reflects severe shortfall consistent with emergency conditions.

Indicator: Quantity of domestic water per capita
Metric: Median litres domestic water per person per day
Remarks/Limitations: Low domestic water indicates hygiene breakdown, potential disease risk
References for threshold: Based on Sphere: Total water (15L) - drinking/cooking (6L) leaves 9L/person/day as the domsetic threshold.

Metric: % of HHs with >100 CFU/100ml at point of use (storage container, etc.)
Remarks/Limitations: Direct exposure risk
References for threshold:
WHO Drinking water quality guidelines. Risk categories: 0 CFU = safe, 1-10 CFU = low risk, 10-100 is intermediate risk, >100 is high risk of contamination. ANA analytical threshold of 40% suggests significant exposure. 
https://www.who.int/teams/environment-climate-change-and-health/water-sanitation-and-health/water-safety-and-quality/drinking-water-quality-guidelines

Indicator: Use of unimproved drinking water sources
Metric: % of HHs relying on unimproved water sources for drinking/cooking water
MSNA indicator: % of households having had access to an improved drinking water source % of households by type of primary source of drinking water (W1)
References for threshold:
Based on JMP service ladder (WHO/UNICEF). ANA analytical threshold indicates widespread use (>80% of HHs, systemic issue). 
https://washdata.org/topics/drinking-water

Indicator: Use of unimproved drinking water sources
Metric: % of HHs relying on surface water as primary water source for drinking and cooking
MSNA indicator: % of households having had access to an improved drinking water source % of households by type of primary source of drinking water (W1)
Remarks/Limitations: Reliance on surface water indicates high vulnerability, increased disease risk
References for threshold:
Based on JMP service ladder (WHO/UNICEF). ANA analytical threshold indicates widespread use (>20% of HHs, significant share of HHs). 
https://washdata.org/topics/drinking-water

Indicator: Qual reports of poor water quality
Metric: Indications that MOST of the population may be reliant on drinking water sources that are exposed to some contamination (e.g. sources of contamination near or at water point including human or animal feces, dead animals, trash or other solid waste, flood waters or other standing/stangant water, etc.)

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of assesssed settlements reported surface water as the most common source of water for drinking and cooking for people in the community

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Hygienic conditions and quality of sanitation faclities
Metric: % of HH with access to unimproved sanitation facilities
MSNA indicator: % of households with access to improved sanitation facilities s% of households using a sanitation facility, by type of sanitation facility used (S1)
Remarks/Limitations: Check contextualized options for unimproved facilities

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Hygienic conditions and quality of sanitation faclities
Metric: % of HHs reporting hygiene related issues with latrines
MSNA indicator: % of households having had problems related to sanitation facility access, by type of problem

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Hygienic conditions and quality of sanitation faclities
Metric: % of HHs reporting protection or safety related issues with latrine
MSNA indicator: % of households having had problems related to sanitation facility access, by type of problem

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of latrines that meet minimum technical requirements

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of latrines that are hygienic and cleaned on a regular basis

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of latrines meeting minimum protection requirements (locks, sex seperated, etc.)

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Quality of SAM Nutrition Services- CMAM performance: (Cure rates, Defaulter rates, Death rates)
Metric: CMAM performance - cure rates-SAM %
References for threshold: Based on SQUEAC/SLEAC (Action Against Hunger / Valid International) coverage standards, and Nutrition Working group: Technical Issue Paper #1 CMAM - specific programme indicators: >75% Cure rate. ANA thresholds reflect insufficient coverage to meet population needs. https://ec.europa.eu/echo/files/policies/sectoral/TIP%20CMAM.pdf

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Quality of SAM Nutrition Services- CMAM performance: (Cure rates, Defaulter rates, Death rates)
Metric: CMAM performance - death rates-SAM %
References for threshold: Based on SQUEAC/SLEAC (Action Against Hunger / Valid International) coverage standards, and Nutrition Working group: Technical Issue Paper #1 CMAM - specific programme indicator: >10% death rate. ANA thresholds reflect insufficient coverage to meet population needs.  https://ec.europa.eu/echo/files/policies/sectoral/TIP%20CMAM.pdf

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Quality of SAM Nutrition Services- CMAM performance: (Cure rates, Defaulter rates, Death rates)
Metric: CMAM performance - defaulter rates- SAM %
References for threshold: ased on SQUEAC/SLEAC (Action Against Hunger / Valid International) coverage standards, and Nutrition Working group: Technical Issue Paper #1 CMAM - specific programme indicators: >15% defaulters. ANA thresholds reflect insufficient coverage to meet population needs. https://ec.europa.eu/echo/files/policies/sectoral/TIP%20CMAM.pdf

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Quality of MAM Nutrition Services- CMAM performance: (Cure rates, Defaulter rates, Death rates)
Metric: CMAM performance - cure rates-MAM%
References for threshold: Less standardised for MAM compared to SAM, threshold reflects similar expectations (see above)

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Quality of MAM Nutrition Services- CMAM performance: (Cure rates, Defaulter rates, Death rates)
Metric: CMAM performance - death rates-MAM %
References for threshold: Less standardised for MAM compared to SAM, threshold reflects lower likelihood of death  among MAM cases compared to SAM cases (see above)

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Quality of MAM Nutrition Services- CMAM performance: (Cure rates, Defaulter rates, Death rates)
Metric: CMAM performance - defaulter rates- MAM %
References for threshold: Less standardised for MAM compared to SAM, threshold reflects similar expectations (see above)

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Accessibility & Availability (Coverage of SC, OTP or TSFP nutrition services.)
Metric: Coverage of SC/ OTP/ SFP in IDP camps
References for threshold:
Sphere minimum standards for perforamnce indicators of coverage: in camp settings suggests >90% coverage as acceptable access. ANA threshold reflects severe access gap. 
https://www.indikit.net/document/17-standardised-indicators-and-categories-for-better-cmam-reporting#:~:text=This%20paper%20outlines%20standardised%20reporting,systems%20to%20fit%20best%20practice.

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Accessibility & Availability (Coverage of SC, OTP or TSFP nutrition services.)
Metric: Coverage of SC/ OTP/ SFP in urban populations
References for threshold: Sphere minimum standards for perforamnce indicators of coverage: in camp settings suggests >70% coverage as acceptable access in urban settings. ANA threshold reflects severe access gap. https://www.indikit.net/document/17-standardised-indicators-and-categories-for-better-cmam-reporting#:~:text=This%20paper%20outlines%20standardised%20reporting,systems%20to%20fit%20best%20practice.

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Accessibility & Availability (Coverage of SC, OTP or TSFP nutrition services.)
Metric: Coverage of SC/ OTP/ SFP in rural populations
References for threshold: Sphere minimum standards for perforamnce indicators of coverage: in camp settings suggests >50% coverage as acceptable access in rural settings. ANA threshold reflects severe access gap. https://www.indikit.net/document/17-standardised-indicators-and-categories-for-better-cmam-reporting#:~:text=This%20paper%20outlines%20standardised%20reporting,systems%20to%20fit%20best%20practice.

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % Measles case fatality rates
References for threshold:
Current estimates used by WHO for CFRs in endemic countries range between 0.05-6%. ANA threshold is set at 5% as a signal of concern. 
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19188207/

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % Cholera case fatality rates
References for threshold: WHO handles an emergency threshold of >1% CFR for Cholera in treatment centres. According to the Global Taskforce on Cholera Control, a CFR >1% is usually due to one or a combination of different factors, such as poor access to health facilities, lack of healthcare-seeking behaviour, and/or inadequate case management. https://www.gtfcc.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/gtfcc-cholera-outbreak-response-field-manual-2024.pdf

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % Ebola case fatality rates
References for threshold:
Ebola has a high baseline CFR, estimations of the Ebola CFR from the years 1976-2022 approach around 60% globally. ANA threshold set at the higher end of baseline estimates. 
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1876034123003581

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of health facilities providing minimum package of primary health services as defined by in country policies
References for threshold:
Based on WHO essential health service frameworks. Threshold reflects reduced availability of core health services.
https://www.who.int/data/gho/indicator-metadata-registry/imr-details/7793#:~:text=IV.&text=Associated%20terms:,and%20the%20most%20disadvantaged%20population.&text=Method%20of%20estimation:,to%20100%20(optimal%20value).&text=Step%203:%20Calculate%20the%20geometric,values%20calculated%20in%20Step%202.

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of health facilties providing basic reproductive health care services (e.g.safe delivery services , emergency obstretic and newborn care, etc.)
References for threshold:
Based on WHO essential health service frameworks. Threshold reflects reduced availability of core health services.
https://www.who.int/data/gho/indicator-metadata-registry/imr-details/7793#:~:text=IV.&text=Associated%20terms:,and%20the%20most%20disadvantaged%20population.&text=Method%20of%20estimation:,to%20100%20(optimal%20value).&text=Step%203:%20Calculate%20the%20geometric,values%20calculated%20in%20Step%202.

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of health facilities providing basic outpatient services
References for threshold:
Based on WHO essential health service frameworks. Threshold reflects reduced availability of core health services.
https://www.who.int/data/gho/indicator-metadata-registry/imr-details/7793#:~:text=IV.&text=Associated%20terms:,and%20the%20most%20disadvantaged%20population.&text=Method%20of%20estimation:,to%20100%20(optimal%20value).&text=Step%203:%20Calculate%20the%20geometric,values%20calculated%20in%20Step%202.

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of health facilities providing basic maternal and newborn health services
References for threshold:
Based on WHO essential health service frameworks. Threshold reflects reduced availability of core health services.
https://www.who.int/data/gho/indicator-metadata-registry/imr-details/7793#:~:text=IV.&text=Associated%20terms:,and%20the%20most%20disadvantaged%20population.&text=Method%20of%20estimation:,to%20100%20(optimal%20value).&text=Step%203:%20Calculate%20the%20geometric,values%20calculated%20in%20Step%202.

Metric: % of people sleeping under an insecticide treated bed net the previous night
% change in cost of mosquito nets
No mosquito nets available in market

Indicator: Qualitative reports of exposure to disease vectors
Metric: Indications that some of the population may have increased exposure to disease vectors due to inadequate shelter or the environment. This may include: (a) standing water as mosquito breeding grounds (b) poor ventilation of shelters(c) contamined or waste water near shelter(d) rats, pests, or other disease carriers

Indications that MOST of the population may be overcrowded and may be facing increased risks for respiratory disease, protection concerns, or mental health distress. Some indications may include:(a) multiple households sharing shelter (b) people cramped into small shared sleeping spaces(c) not all household members can lie down or fit in the sleeping space(d) reports of widespread coughing or respiratory issues (e) reports of tensions or fighting between neighbours due to proximity and lack of privacy (f) long lines or waits for communal facilities such as latrines, water, etc.

Indicator: Overall infectious disease incidence (all cause and all age)
Metric: # of consulations/treatments for infectious diseases per 1000 people per epiweek

Indicator: Any unmet health needs in past three months
Metric: % of individuals with unmet health needs in the past 3 months
MSNA indicator: % of individuals with a healthcare need

Metric: Average covered living space per person
References for threshold: Based on Sphere minimum standards: Minimum 3.5 m2 of living space per person, excluding cooking space, bathing area and sanitation facility. https://spherestandards.org/wp-content/uploads/Sphere-in-Practice.pdf

Metric: % of HHs with at least 3.5 m2 of covered living space per person
References for threshold: Based on Sphere minimum standards: Minimum 3.5 m2 of living space per person, ANA threshold suggests significant population facing gap.

Metric: Deaths per 10,000 per day
MSNA indicator: # of deaths per 10,000 people per day and / or # of under-5 year old deaths per 10,000 under-5 children per day
Remarks/Limitations: Need denominator
References for threshold: Well-established emergency threshold (WHO, Shpere, UNHCR Emergency Handbook) https://emergency.unhcr.org/emergency-assistance/health-and-nutrition/mortality-surveillance-threshold

Metric: Deaths of under fives per 10,000 per day
MSNA indicator: # of deaths per 10,000 people per day and / or # of under-5 year old deaths per 10,000 under-5 children per day
Remarks/Limitations: Need denominator
References for threshold: Well-established emergency threshold (WHO, Shpere, UNHCR Emergency Handbook) https://emergency.unhcr.org/emergency-assistance/health-and-nutrition/mortality-surveillance-threshold

Metric: # of confirmed or suspected cases of measles in children 6 months to 15 years of age (or context specific age groups), where at least one is confirmed
References for threshold: WHO defines suspected measles outbreak as five or more epidemiologically linked cases, and confirmed outbreak as two or more lab-confirmed linked cases. This threshold is adapted for the ANA to flag concern.  https://www.who.int/emergencies/outbreak-toolkit/disease-outbreak-toolboxes/measles-outbreak-toolbox

Metric: # of cases of measles in children 6 months to 15 years of age per 1000 children, per epiweek
Remarks/Limitations: At this level, measles transmission is likely widespread, with high exposure among susceptible children and increased risk of complications and excess mortality in contexts with malnutrition or weak health systems.
References for threshold:
ANA analytical threshold in the absence of an overall global standard threshold. Based on the Sphere Handbook, WHO outbreak response guidelines, MSF field thresholds 
https://medicalguidelines.msf.org/sites/default/files/pdf/guideline-498-en.pdf

Indicator: Reports of measles outbreaks from disease surveillance systems, rapid assessments, or other secondary sources
Metric: Confirmed measles outbreak, not controlled (cases increasing weekly, see epidemic curve)
References for threshold: ANA analytical threshold, binary threshold is analytical rather than global severity cut-off. See WHO disease-specific toolboxes https://www.who.int/emergencies/outbreak-toolkit/disease-outbreak-toolboxes

Metric: # of cases of cholera/suspect cholera in populations where at least one case is confirmed
References for threshold: WHO/GTFCC define a suspected cholera outbreak when two or more suspected cholera cases or one suspected case with positive RDT result are reported within one surveillance unit within 7 days. This threshold is adapted for the ANA to flag outbreak severity. https://www.gtfcc.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/gtfcc-cholera-outbreak-response-field-manual-2024.pdf

Metric: # of cases of cholera/suspect cholera per 1000 people per week (all ages)
References for threshold: ANA analytical threshold in the absence of an overall global standard threshold. Based on the Sphere Handbook, WHO outbreak response guidelines, MSF field thresholds

Indicator: Reports of cholera outbreaks from disease surveillance systems, rapid assessments, or other secondary sources
Metric: Confirmed outbreak, not controlled (cases increasing weekly, see epidemic curve)

Indicator: Reports of possible spikes in infectious diseases e.g. from expert health KII
Metric: Reports of large increases in malaria, diarrhoeal disease, respiratory infectionsor other infectious disease not attributable to pre-crisis conditions or seasonality

Indicator: Reports of suspected measles outbreak e.g. from expert health KII
Metric: Qualitative reports of children with cough + neck rashes + fevers as suspect measles cases

Indicator: Reports of suspected cholera outbreak e.g. from expert health KII
Metric: Qualitative reports of adults with serious cases of acute watery diarrhoea

Indicator: Reports of increasing health needs e.g. from expert health KII
Metric: Qualitative reports of an increase in health needs in the community (e.g. overcrowded health facilities, community events or normal activities of the community disrupted due to illnesses, increased number of deaths in the community, etc.)

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of monitored items or categories for which # days of remaining stock â # days needed to restock < 1

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of interviewed vendors reporting difficulties keeping their business operational and well-stocked over the last X days/months

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of interviewed vendors mostly relying on a single supplier to stock their businesses (by category)

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: Full reported MFS by assessed market
% of HHs with inadequate shelter
MSNA indicator: % of households by type of shelter they currently live in
Remarks/Limitations: Check contextualized options for inadequate shelter type
References for threshold: Based on Sphere shelter standards (shelter must provide protection from climate, safety, and privacy). Threshold reflects a substantial share of households lacking adequate protection from environmental conditions; definition of inadequacy is context-specific.

Indicator: Cost of tarpaulin or other key shelter materials
Metric: % change in tarpaulin or other shelter materials

Indicator: Cost of tarpaulin or other key shelter materials
Metric: No tarpaulin (or other key shelter material) available in market

Indications that MOST of the population may be living in inadequate shelter that either (a) is not durable or sturdy (b) does not provide adequate privacy (c) does not provide safety or protection from the weather/elements (d) does not provide safe place to sleep
MDD-W => 5 food groups at least
References for threshold:
Aligned with FAO-MDD-W guidelines (standard is ≥5 of 10 food groups)
https://www.fao.org/nutrition/assessment/tools/minimum-dietary-diversity-women/en/

Indicator: Qual reports of populations struggling to meet water needs
Metric: Indications that most of the population is struggling to access their minimum water needs. (e.g. reported long queues, fighting at water points, spikes in drinking water prices, use of livelihood coping strategies to buy water)

Indicator: Incidence of common childhood illnesses and symptoms per epiweek
Metric: # of cases (all-causes) of common childhood illness in children under-5 years of age per 1000, per epiweek

Indicator: Incidence of common childhood illnesses and symptoms per epiweek
Metric: # of cases of acute respiratory illness in children under-5 years of age per 1000, per epiweek

Indicator: Incidence of common childhood illnesses and symptoms per epiweek
Metric: # of cases of acute watery diarrhoea in children under-5 years of age per 1000, per epiweek
% of children under-5 years with any illness in the two weeks prior to data collection
MSNA indicator: % of children under-5 years of age sick in the last two weeks as reported by primary caregiver
References for threshold: ANA analytical threshold, to be interpreted against seasonality, baseline incidence, surveillance quality, and triangulated evidence of service strain or outbreak escalation.

Metric: % of children under-5 years with any acute respiratory infection in the two weeks prior to data collection
MSNA indicator: % of children under-5 years of age sick in the last two weeks as reported by primary caregiver, by symptom
Remarks/Limitations: Not confirmed but can obtain symptomology
References for threshold: ANA analytical threshold, to be interpreted against seasonality, baseline incidence, surveillance quality, and triangulated evidence of service strain or outbreak escalation.

Metric: % of children under-5 years with any acute watery diarrhoea in the two weeks prior to data collection
MSNA indicator: % of children under-5 years of age sick in the last two weeks as reported by primary caregiver, by symptom
Remarks/Limitations: Not confirmed but can obtain symptomology
References for threshold: ANA analytical threshold, to be interpreted against seasonality, baseline incidence, surveillance quality, and triangulated evidence of service strain or outbreak escalation.

Indicator: Qualitative reports on main food groups consumed
Metric: Qualitative reports on 1-2 food groups remaining (staple cereals, pulses or else) in the area, possibly only through in-kind assistance, hot meals distribution or wild foods gathering
% HHs with significant decrease in average # of months of food stocks

Indicator: Availability of monitored staple cereals in the market
Metric: # of monitored staple cereals for which limited availability or no availability is reported
% of localities where very insufficient or no food available % of localities where not enough food is available on markets
% of localities or share of pop in a locality where access to farmlandand/or pasture is not possible or extremely limited

Indicator: Qualitative reports on inavailability of food
Metric: Qualitative reports that key food sources are depleted or extremely low (local crops destroyed, flooded, uncultivated OR markets without food), and/or alterative food sources are mainly providing irregular amounts of food to the area (wild foods, aid, etc.)

Indicator: Qualitative reports on depleted food stocks
Metric: Qualitative reports on food stocks (local or imported crops, warehouses stocks, etc.) at area level being depleted or (rural areas) that households' stocks are depleted (most often local crops).

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % HHs with [significant] decrease in livestock production
MSNA indicator: How many {fsl_livestock_type} did you have at the same time last year? How many {fsl_livestock_type} do you have now?

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % HHs planting less than previous season
MSNA indicator: % of households planting less than $reference year$

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % HHs with low harvest expectation
MSNA indicator: % of households by harvest expectation

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: Agricultural Stress Index (ASI)

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: Decrease in (ha) cultivated compared to previous same season

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of localities where there has been a decrease in livestock (more than half at least)

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of localities where crop production quantity from the last harvest was much less or zero compared to previous harvest
% HHs with HWISE-4
MSNA module: Drinking water - HWISE-12 Drinking water - HWISE-12 / Urban
References for threshold:
Based on HWISE scale; no globally agreed severity cut-offs exist. ANA threshold reflects widespread household water insecurity (>50%). 
https://www.rural-water-supply.net/en/resources/991-

Indicator: Water availability at main water source
Metric: Average number of days per week of water availability at main water source
MSNA indicator: % of households by number of days water was unavailable from main source in the past month (XW8)
References for threshold:
Sphere minimum standard reflects reliable daily access to water. ANA threshold here to indicate acutely severe availability gap. 
https://spherestandards.org/wp-content/uploads/Sphere-Handbook-2018-EN.pdf

Indicator: Water availability at main water source
Metric: % of households with 4 days or less per week of water availability at main water source
MSNA indicator: % of households by number of days water was unavailable from main source in the past month (XW8)
Remarks/Limitations: Question asks about the past month but can perhaps proxy from this
References for threshold:
Sphere minimum standard reflects reliable daily access to water. ANA threshold here to indicate significant proportion of the population affected by service gap. 
https://spherestandards.org/wp-content/uploads/Sphere-Handbook-2018-EN.pdf

Metric: % of HHs reporting they use less preferred, unimproved or unsafe water sources to cope with lack of drinking water
MSNA indicator: % of households engaging in coping mechanisms due water insufficiency, by type of coping mechanism
References for threshold: ANA threshold, reflecting significant proportion of population using severe coping mechanism.

Metric: % of HHs reporting reducing water usage for hygiene to cope with lack of water
MSNA indicator: % of households engaging in coping mechanisms due water insufficiency, by type of coping mechanism
References for threshold: ANA threshold, reflecting majority of population using severe coping mechanism.

Metric: % of HHs reporting [other severe context specific coping] to cope with lack of water
MSNA indicator: % of households engaging in coping mechanisms due water insufficiency, by type of coping mechanism
References for threshold: ANA threshold, reflecting majority of population using severe coping mechanism.

Indicator: Qual reports of stressed availability of water at HH level
Metric: Indications that MOST of the popluation may be experiencing household level stress on availability of water at the household level (inadequate storage for water preventing adequate water consumption, water service interruptions, etc.)

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Sufficient number of functional water points
Metric: # of people per tap stand

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Sufficient number of functional water points

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: Number of people per primary health facility catchment area
Remarks/Limitations: Right to health imposes four essential standards on healthcare services: Availabiltiy, Accessibility, Acceptability, and Quality (AAAQ), from WHO. Indicators used here reflect this framework. https://www.who.int/news-room/questions-and-answers/item/q-a-on-the-health-workforce-crisis#:~:text=What%20do%20we%20mean%20by,outcomes%20will%20not%20be%20satisfactory.
References for threshold:
WHO and national planning norms often benchmark primary care facility catchment as 10,000 - 20,000 per PHC. UNHCR Emergency Handbook describes 10,000 people per basic health unit in the emergency phase. 
https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789240044210 
https://emergency.unhcr.org/emergency-assistance/health-and-nutrition/primary-health-care/primary-health-care-coverage-standard

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: Number of people per secondary health facility (hospitals)

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: Number of inpatient beds per 10,000 people
References for threshold:
Sphere handbook specifies a minimum of 18 beds / 10,0000 persons (excluding maternity beds) to ensure adequate coverage during emergencies. ANA threshold indicates severe gap. 
http://www.humanitarianstandardspartnership.org/ViewContent?DocID=1000011&VersionID=2000017&ChapterNumber=8&OrderInChapter=6&Lang=en

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of functional primary health care (PHC) facilities
References for threshold: No gblobal thresholds exist, "functionality* states can be based on WHO HeRAMS or other health facility monitoring. ANA threshold reflects functionality levels consistent with major health system disruption.

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of functional secondary health care (SHC) facilities
References for threshold: No gblobal thresholds exist, "functionality* states can be based on WHO HeRAMS or other health facility monitoring. ANA threshold reflects functionality levels consistent with major health system disruption.

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of facilities reporting lack of supplies as a main functionality constraint

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: Number of skilled birth attendants per 10000 people
References for threshold:
Based on WHO and UNICEF frameworks for skilled birth attendance and maternal health workforce capacity. No global density thresholds exist; ANA thresholds reflect severe constraints in availability of skilled maternal health personnel.
 https://data.unicef.org/indicator-profiles/MNCH_SAB/

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: Number of health workers per 10000 people
References for threshold:
Based on WHO health workforce density benchmarks (44.5 per 10,000 for universal health coverage). ANA threshold reflects severe shortages relative to global standards.
https://www.who.int/data/gho/indicator-metadata-registry/imr-details/health-workers-density-(per-10-000-population)--by-occupation#:~:text=The%20SDG%20agenda%20gives%20recognition,Resolution%20(WHA69.

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Metric: % of facilities reporting lack of staffing as a main functionality constraint

Metric: % of monitored items for which no availability is reported

Level: Community or system level contributing factors
Indicator: Level of availability of monitored items during the data collection window (by category of item)
Metric: % of monitored items for which limited OR no availability is reported
% of children 6-59 months GAM by WHZ
Remarks/Limitations: If data covers all chidlren 0-59 months, value would slightly over-estimate acute malnutrition, as children 0-6 months are usually be breastfed and are likely to be flagged as acuteley malnourished by WHO standards. Extra caution is advised.
References for threshold: Recommended threshold according to IPC Acute Malnutrition (2018). GAM 15-29.9% refers to IPC AMN Critical phase (4).

Metric: % of children 6-59 months GAM by MUAC
Remarks/Limitations: Prevalence of GAM by MUAC should be referred to as “proxy” GAM, as it is not a true representative indicator of malnutrition in the population. This is because MUAC measurements are more sensitive younger children (under 2 years of age) and will likely not detect older children that are malnourished by the WHZ criteria.
References for threshold: IPC Guidance suggests usign MUAC only when WHZ is not available. 10-15% GAM by MUAC corresponds roughly to IPC AMN Phase 3 and 4. Acute Needs threshold is therefore slightly conservative.

Metric: % of children 6-59 months combined GAM
% change of monthly new SAM admissions at Stabilization Center or OTP programs
% change of monthly defaulters to SC or OTP programs
% change of monthly deaths reported in Stabilization Centers or OTP

Indicator: Trends of acute malnutriton in the last 3 months
Metric: Qualitative reports of doubled incidence of acute malnutrition in locality/cathcment area in last 3m.

Indicator: Stock-outs of RUTF/ RUSF nutrition supplements/products in the last 3 months
Metric: Qualitative reports of stock-outs of RUTF/ RUSF nutrition supplements/products in the last 3 months (% stock-out)

Indicator: Number of community outreach activities, refferals and community screening of malnutrition by community health workers in catchment area in last 1 month.
Metric: Qualitative reports of number of community outreach, referrals and community screenings of malnutrition in last 1 month
% HHs mainly relying on atypical food sources
MSNA indicator: % of households by main sources of food in the 30 days prior to data collection
Remarks/Limitations:
Atypical should be defined per context. Default options include: Borrowed
Gift from family and/or friends
Begging
References for threshold: Based on IPC and WFP coping strategy frameworks. “Atypical” food sources are context-specific and include socially or economically unsustainable sources (e.g. borrowing, begging). Threshold reflects widespread reliance on crisis coping.

Metric: % of HHs reporting emergency level livelihoods based coping strategies
MSNA indicator: Livelihood Coping Strategy - Essential Needs (LCS-EN)

Metric: % change in local food prices against previous year and five-year average (incl. seasonality)
% change in ToTs for staple foods against previous year and five-year average, incl. seasonality
HEA outcome - survival deficit (households cannot meet 50% of their minimum food and non-food survival needs without irreversible coping).)
Remarks/Limitations: Survival deficit = inability to meet minimum food + non-food needs without irreversible coping
References for threshold: Based on Household Economy Approach (HEA). Survival deficit indicates households cannot meet minimum food and non-food needs without irreversible coping.

Metric: % of HHs relying on atypical or"unstable" sources of income (exchange for labor, gifts, begging, etc.)
MSNA indicator: Household income over the 30 days prior to data collection, by amount and % from each source Most common sources of household income over the 30 days prior to data collection

Metric: % of localities with a big increase in food prices in the last 30 days
% of localities where at least half of the pop has very limited access to food
% of localities where at least half of the pop do very unusual and desperate things just to obtain a small amount of food
% HH for whom food is 75% of expenditures
MSNA indicator: Household expenditures in the 30 days prior to data collection, by amount and % per type

% of HHs with travel and queue time to water source more than 30 minutes
MSNA indicator: % of households by time (minutes) taken to fetch water (round trip by walking, queuing and time needed to fetch water) (W3/W4)
References for threshold: Sphere minimum standard = ≤30 minutes round trip. ANA threshold suggests significant share of population unable to make this roundtrip within that timeframe. https://spherestandards.org/wp-content/uploads/Sphere-Handbook-2018-EN.pdf

Metric: % of HHs spending more than 5% of income on water
MSNA indicator: Household expenditures in the 30 days prior to data collection, by amount and % per type
References for threshold: No global threshold exists. 3-5% is often used as affordability threshold. The UN Resolution on Human Rights in Drinking Water and Sewerage (24 March 2011) sets the threshold at 5%, elsewhere 3% is reported (e.g., https://www.un.org/en/global-issues/water#:~:text=One%20of%20the%20most%20important,should%20not%20exceed%2030%20minutes.)

Metric: % of HHs reporting physical barriers to accessing water points
MSNA indicator: % of households having had problems related to access to water, by type of problem

Metric: % of HHs reporting financial access barriers to accessing water points
MSNA indicator: % of households having had problems related to access to water, by type of problem

Indicator: Qual reports of extreme water coping strategies
Metric: Indications that MOST of the population may be resorting to extreme coping behaviours to meet their daily water needs (spending significant daily time to fetch water each day, using unsafe or contaminated water sources, displacement due to lack of water, etc.)

Indicator: Qual reports of limited physical access to water
Metric: Indications that MOST of the popluation may be facing serious physical access constraints to accessing their main drinking water sources (insecurity or lack of safety on the way to or at the water point, flooding or other natural hazard cutting off access, etc.)

Indicator: Qual reports of limited financial access to water
Metric: Indications that some of the popluation may be facing serious financial access constraints to accessing their main drinking water sources (extreme price increases double or triple the normal price, selling of assets to afford water, etc.)
